hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Talking About Your World -- Trips & Highlights
lesson.menu_desc: Describing past experiences, trips and professional highlights -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Talking About Your World
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 4 -- Talking About Your World

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is Ricardo. I wanted to tell you how the trip to Portugal went last week. Honestly, it turned out even better than I expected. We visited six hotels in three days, and I did a full inspection of each one. The highlight was a small property near Porto with amazing rates for next season. The negotiation was tough at first, but in the end we signed a great contract with a fifteen percent commission. The biggest challenge was the schedule, because we drove almost four hundred kilometers, but it was worth it. I ended up with three new partners for our itineraries. Give me a call when you are free, and I will send you all the details.

Good afternoon, everyone. Before we finish for the week, I want to share a quick recap of the fair. Overall, it was a real success. Our team held more than forty meetings, and we met several new tour operators from Europe. The highlight was the panel on sustainable travel, which gave us many fresh ideas. We faced one big challenge: two of our biggest suppliers could not attend, so we rescheduled those meetings for next month. Even so, the outcome was excellent. We ended up with eight strong leads and two signed contracts. Thank you all for your hard work, and please send me your trip reports by Friday.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the final result of an action or event
the best or most memorable part of an experience
the percentage a company earns on each sale
something good you succeeded in doing
a visit to check a hotel's quality
a difficult task that tests your skills
a company that creates and sells travel packages

reading.rtitle: The Trip That Changed the Season
paras: Last November, Andrea attended WTM in London, one of the biggest travel fairs in the world. She went with a clear goal: to find new hotel partners for the agency's European itineraries. The first two days were a real challenge. The halls were huge, the schedule was tight, and many of the exhibitors she wanted to meet were fully booked.

On the third day, everything changed. Andrea met Ricardo, a tour operator from Lisbon, and they immediately found common ground. He invited her on a short fam trip to Portugal the following week. There, she did a full hotel inspection of six properties, negotiated the rates, and agreed on a fair commission. The negotiation was tough, but in the end it turned out perfectly.

By the time she flew home, Andrea had signed three new contracts. The highlight, she says, was not the paperwork. It was the moment she realized her English no longer got in the way. 'A year ago, I would have stayed quiet at that fair,' she says. 'This time, I talked, I asked, I negotiated -- and I ended up with the best results of my career.'
reading.source: Adapted from a travel-industry profile, Voices in Tourism

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Andrea's trip was a failure because the fair was too big and busy.
A tough trip turned into Andrea's biggest success -- and proved her English was ready.
Andrea decided to stop attending international fairs after WTM.

items: Andrea attended WTM in London last November.
The first sentence states this directly.
The first two days of the fair were easy and relaxed for Andrea.
The text says the first two days were a real challenge -- huge halls and a tight schedule.
Andrea did a hotel inspection of six properties in Portugal.
On the fam trip she inspected six properties and negotiated the rates.
Andrea flew home without signing any contracts.
By the time she flew home, she had signed three new contracts.

parts: WTM in London. The first days were a real
. Then she met a tour operator and
up on a fam trip to Portugal. The negotiation
of the whole trip was signing three new contracts.

gapfill.text: Notice the past forms: regular verbs add -ed (attended, signed, ended, turned), while many common verbs are irregular (go -> went, meet -> met, do -> did, fly -> flew). 'Turn out' means to happen in a certain way in the end; 'end up' means to finally be in a place or situation.

lf.text: Notice: regular verbs (sign -> signed, turn -> turned) simply add -ed. Irregular verbs change completely (go -> went, meet -> met, do -> did). In questions and negatives, the past tense moves to 'did': Did you sign the contract? -- not 'Did you signed'.

bank.label: Useful language for telling a story
items: At first, it was a challenge, but...
The highlight of the trip was...
In the end, it turned out...
Looking back, my main achievement was...
Tell the story of a real fair or trip you attended. Say where you went, what you did, and who you met -- all in the past simple.
Describe the biggest challenge on that trip and how it turned out in the end.
Ask a colleague three past-simple questions about their last trip: Did you...? How did...? Was it...?
Share one professional achievement from last year and explain the outcome in three sentences.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = attended, 2 = challenge, 3 = ended, 4 = turned, 5 = highlight.
Model story: 'Last November I attended WTM. At first it was a challenge, but on the third day I met a tour operator and ended up on a fam trip to Portugal.'
Model challenge: 'The negotiation was tough at first, but in the end it turned out perfectly and we signed the contract.'
Past questions: Did you sign the contract? How did the meeting go? Was it your first time there?
answerkey.note: Use the past simple for finished actions at a finished time. In questions and negatives, use 'did' + the base verb: 'Did you meet...?', not 'Did you met...?'.
